{{Short description|Canadian-American clinical psychologist}}
{{Use dmy dates|date=April 2022}}

[[File:Cindy Meston.jpg|thumb]]
'''Cindy Meston''' is a Canadian-American [[Clinical psychology|clinical psychologist]] well-known for her research<ref>{{Cite web|title=Google Scholar|url=https://scholar.google.com/scholar?hl=en&as_sdt=0,46&q=cindy+meston&btnG=|access-date=2021-01-04|website=scholar.google.com}}</ref> on the psychophysiology of female [[sexual arousal]]. She is a Full Professor of Clinical Psychology at the [[University of Texas at Austin]], Director of the ''Female Sexual Psychophysiology Laboratory'',<ref>{{Cite web|title=MESTONLAB.COM|url=http://www.mestonlab.com/|access-date=2021-01-04|website=www.mestonlab.com}}</ref> and author of ''Why Women Have Sex'' <ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2009-09-27|title=Why women have sex|url=http://www.theguardian.com/lifeandstyle/2009/sep/28/sex-women-relationships-tanya-gold|access-date=2021-01-04|website=the Guardian|language=en}}</ref> (with co-author [[David Buss|Dr. David M. Buss]]). In 2016, the BBC, London, [[England]] named Meston one of the [[100 Women (BBC)|100]] most influential and inspirational women in the world.<ref name="BBC">{{cite news|last=Keane|first=Phoebe|date=2 December 2016|title=100 Women 2016: Researching the female orgasm|newspaper=BBC World Service|publisher=BBC|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/magazine-38170324|access-date=10 December 2016}}</ref>

==Early life==
Meston was born in Abbotsford, [[British Columbia]], Canada.<ref name="utexas1">{{cite web|url=http://labs.la.utexas.edu/mestonlab/files/2016/05/meston-Curriculum-Vitae-1-13-16.pdf |title=Cindy May Meston: CV |website=Labs.la.utexas.edu |access-date=2016-12-10}}</ref> Prior to becoming a noted sex researcher, Meston lived in Vancouver, B.C. where she worked in [[fashion design]] and then as the ''Western Canadian Sewing Specialist'' for [[Elna (Swiss company)|White/Elna Sewing Machine Company]].

==Education and career==
Meston received a Ph.D. in Clinical Psychology from the [[University of British Columbia]] in 1995. She completed most of her postgraduate training at the [[University of Washington]] Medical Center in the departments of Sexual and Reproductive Medicine, Psychiatry, and Urology.<ref name="utexas1"/> From 1996-1998 she received a Fellowship from the [[Ford Foundation]] in New York to study the effects of early childhood sexual abuse on adult sexual function. In 1998, she accepted a position as Assistant Professor at the [[University of Texas at Austin]] and was promoted to Full Professor of Clinical Psychology in 2007.<ref name=":1" />

Meston has published over 200 [[Peer review|peer-reviewed]] articles and book chapters and given over 300 conference presentations on women’s sexuality.<ref name="utexas1" /><ref>{{Cite web|title=Cindy Meston on why people have sex|url=https://www.ideacity.ca/video/cindy-meston-people-sex/|access-date=2021-01-04|website=ideacity|language=en-US}}</ref> Her book, ''Why Women Have Sex'' (co-authored with [[David Buss|Dr. David Buss]]) has been translated into nine languages and has received extensive media coverage.<ref name=":0" /><ref name="TNY">{{cite news|newspaper=The New Yorker|title=Ask an Academic: Why Women Have Sex|url=http://www.newyorker.com/books/page-turner/ask-an-academic-why-women-have-sex|first=Sally |last=Law|date=1 December 2009|access-date=10 December 2016}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.penguin.co.uk/authors/cindy-meston/1064443/|title=Cindy Meston|website=Penguin.co.uk|access-date=2016-12-08|archive-date=21 December 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161221083653/https://www.penguin.co.uk/authors/cindy-meston/1064443/|url-status=dead}}</ref> Articles on her research and book have appeared in over 300 newspapers (e.g., ''Science Times, [[The New York Times|New York Times]]''), magazines (e.g., ''[[The New Yorker|NewYorker]]'', ''[[Cosmopolitan (magazine)|Cosmopolitan]]'', ''[[Harper's Bazaar|Harper’s Bazaar]]'', ''[[Elle (magazine)|Elle]]'', ''[[Glamour (magazine)|Glamour]]''), and online publications.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=Cindy M. Meston, Ph.D.|url=https://labs.la.utexas.edu/mestonlab/cindy-meston-ph-d/|access-date=2021-01-04|website=labs.la.utexas.edu}}</ref> She has made several television appearances (e.g., ''[[Rachael Ray (talk show)|The Rachel Ray Show]]'', ''[[Dr. Phil (talk show)|The Dr. Phil Show]]'', [[ABC News (United States)|ABC]] (''20/20''), [[NBC News|NBC]] National News) and conducted over 50 national radio interviews (e.g., [[NPR]]) and podcasts.<ref name=":1" /> Meston is also a co-author (with Irwin Goldstein, Susan Davis, and Abulmaged Traish) of the book ''Women’s Sexual Function and Dysfunction,'' <ref name=":2">{{Cite web|title=Women's Sexual Function and Dysfunction: Study, Diagnosis and Treatment|url=https://www.routledge.com/Womens-Sexual-Function-and-Dysfunction-Study-Diagnosis-and-Treatment/Goldstein-Meston-Davis-Traish/p/book/9781842142639|access-date=2021-01-04|website=Routledge & CRC Press|language=en}}</ref> which was published in 2006''.''

She is a past president of the ''International Society for the Study of Women’s Sexual Health''<ref>{{Cite web|title=ISSWSH - Home|url=https://www.isswsh.org/|access-date=2021-01-04|website=www.isswsh.org}}</ref> and an elected member of the ''International Academy of Sex Research'',<ref>{{Cite web|title=IASR|url=http://www.iasrsite.org/|access-date=2021-01-04|website=IASR|language=en-US}}</ref> the ''Society for the Scientific Study of Sex,''<ref>{{Cite web|title=Home - The Society for the Scientific Study of Sexuality|url=https://www.sexscience.org/|access-date=2021-01-04|website=www.sexscience.org}}</ref> and the ''Association for Psychological Science''.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Homepage|url=https://www.psychologicalscience.org/|access-date=2021-01-04|website=Association for Psychological Science - APS|language=en-US}}</ref>

In 2005, Meston was selected as Chair of the orgasm committee for the [[World Health Organization]].<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Meston|first1=Cindy M.|last2=Hull|first2=Elaine|last3=Levin|first3=Roy J.|last4=Sipski|first4=Marca|date=July 2004|title=Disorders of orgasm in women|journal=The Journal of Sexual Medicine|volume=1|issue=1|pages=66–68|doi=10.1111/j.1743-6109.2004.10110.x|issn=1743-6095|pmid=16422985}}</ref> The express goal of the committee was to develop an operational definition of female orgasm. The definition developed by Meston and colleagues is still in active use by the W.H.O. Meston has served as a consultant to the ''[[Food and Drug Administration|U.S. Food and Drug Administration]]'' and to numerous pharmaceutical companies who are developing drugs to enhance women’s sexual function.

Awards for Meston’s research include a fellowship from ''[[Ford Foundation|The Ford Foundation]]'', an International Research Award from the ''Athena Institute for Women’s Wellness'', a Distinguished Professor Award from the ''Canadian Research Foundation,'' the ''Raymond Dickson Centennial Endowed Teaching Fellowship'', the Wulf H. Utian Endowed Lecturer Award from the ''North American Menopause Society'', and the Career Service Award from the ''International Society for the Study of Women’s Sexual Health''. She has won ten awards for best peer-reviewed manuscript by international academic societies.<ref name="utexas1" /> She is an elected Fellow of the [[Society for the Scientific Study of Sexuality]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Fellow of The Society |url=https://sexscience.org/content.aspx?page_id=22&club_id=173936&module_id=399670 |access-date=2025-08-09 |website=The Society for the Scientific Study of Sexuality |language=en}}</ref>

==Selected research areas==
'''''Female Sexual Arousal and the Sympathetic Nervous System:'''''  Historically, clinicians, researchers, and theorists believed the early stages of arousal in women were facilitated by the [[parasympathetic nervous system]] (PNS) and inhibited by the [[sympathetic nervous system]] (SNS). While this relationship had never been tested empirically in women, it was assumed to be true based on analogous research on the erectile response in men. Meston was the first researcher to directly examine the role of the SNS in the [[female sexual response]], conducting a series of experiments on humans and animals and finding that, contrary to popular thought, SNS activation does not simply impair sexual arousal in women.<ref name=":3">{{Cite journal|last1=Meston|first1=Cindy M.|last2=Gorzalka|first2=Boris B.|date=1995-07-01|title=The effects of sympathetic activation on physiological and subjective sexual arousal in women|url=https://dx.doi.org/10.1016%2F0005-7967%2895%2900006-J|journal=Behaviour Research and Therapy|language=en|volume=33|issue=6|pages=651–664|doi=10.1016/0005-7967(95)00006-J|pmid=7654157|issn=0005-7967|url-access=subscription}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Meston|first1=Cindy M.|last2=Gorzalka|first2=Boris B.|date=1996-02-01|title=The effects of immediate, delayed, and residual sympathetic activation on sexual arousal in women|url=https://dx.doi.org/10.1016%2F0005-7967%2895%2900050-X|journal=Behaviour Research and Therapy|language=en|volume=34|issue=2|pages=143–148|doi=10.1016/0005-7967(95)00050-X|pmid=8741721|issn=0005-7967|url-access=subscription}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Meston|first1=C. M|last2=Gorzalka|first2=B. B.|date=1996|title=Differential effects of sympathetic activation on sexual arousal in sexually dysfunctional and functional women|url=https://pascal-francis.inist.fr/vibad/index.php?action=getRecordDetail&idt=3260121|journal=Journal of Abnormal Psychology|volume=105|issue=4|pages=582–591|doi=10.1037/0021-843X.105.4.582|pmid=8952191|issn=0021-843X|url-access=subscription}}</ref><ref name=":4">{{Cite journal|last1=Rellini|first1=Alessandra H.|last2=Meston|first2=Cindy M.|date=2006-01-01|title=Psychophysiological Sexual Arousal in Women with a History of Child Sexual Abuse|url=https://doi.org/10.1080/00926230500229145|journal=Journal of Sex & Marital Therapy|volume=32|issue=1|pages=5–22|doi=10.1080/00926230500229145|pmid=16234222|s2cid=16678158|issn=0092-623X|url-access=subscription}}</ref> Rather, Meston’s research suggests that optimal SNS activation seems to be ''necessary'' for the development of female physiological sexual arousal. Meston has conducted numerous follow-up studies examining how these results vary in women with autonomic impairments as a function of anxiety,<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Bradford|first1=Andrea|last2=Meston|first2=Cindy M.|date=2006-08-01|title=The impact of anxiety on sexual arousal in women|url= |journal=Behaviour Research and Therapy|language=en|volume=44|issue=8|pages=1067–1077|doi=10.1016/j.brat.2005.08.006|pmid=16199003|issn=0005-7967|pmc=2857771}}</ref> [[post-traumatic stress disorder]],<ref name=":4" /> and antidepressant usage.<ref name=":5">{{Cite journal|last1=Lorenz|first1=Tierney A.|last2=Meston|first2=Cindy M.|date=June 2012|title=Acute exercise improves physical sexual arousal in women taking antidepressants|journal=Annals of Behavioral Medicine |volume=43|issue=3|pages=352–361|doi=10.1007/s12160-011-9338-1|issn=1532-4796|pmc=3422071|pmid=22403029}}</ref> Most recently, Meston has explored the overall balance between the SNS and PNS (i.e., heart rate variability) as an important marker and treatment target for female sexual function.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Stanton|first1=Amelia M.|last2=Lorenz|first2=Tierney A.|last3=Pulverman|first3=Carey S.|last4=Meston|first4=Cindy M.|date=September 2015|title=Heart Rate Variability: A Risk Factor for Female Sexual Dysfunction|journal=Applied Psychophysiology and Biofeedback|volume=40|issue=3|pages=229–237|doi=10.1007/s10484-015-9286-9|issn=1090-0586|pmc=6711474|pmid=26081002}}</ref>

'''''Sexual Motivation:''''' Few researchers have historically examined sexual motives in women, assuming such motives were likely self-explanatory: for pleasure, for procreation, for love. Together with evolutionary psychologist David Buss, Meston questioned the simplicity of these assumptions and conducted a large-scale study of sexual motives, finding that women actually report 237 distinct reasons for having sex that range from experience-seeking to mate-guarding.<ref name=":6">{{Cite journal|last1=Meston|first1=Cindy M.|last2=Buss|first2=David M.|date=2007-08-01|title=Why Humans Have Sex|url=https://doi.org/10.1007/s10508-007-9175-2|journal=Archives of Sexual Behavior|language=en|volume=36|issue=4|pages=477–507|doi=10.1007/s10508-007-9175-2|pmid=17610060|s2cid=6182053|issn=1573-2800|url-access=subscription}}</ref> Meston has also conducted a series of follow-up studies finding that such motives vary based on sexual satisfaction levels <ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Stephenson|first1=Kyle R.|last2=Ahrold|first2=Tierney K.|last3=Meston|first3=Cindy M.|date=2011-06-01|title=The Association Between Sexual Motives and Sexual Satisfaction: Gender Differences and Categorical Comparisons|url= |journal=Archives of Sexual Behavior|language=en|volume=40|issue=3|pages=607–618|doi=10.1007/s10508-010-9674-4|issn=1573-2800|pmc=3038192|pmid=20967494}}</ref> and age.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Meston|first1=Cindy M.|last2=Hamilton|first2=Lisa Dawn|last3=Harte|first3=Christopher B.|date=2009-12-01|title=Sexual Motivation in Women as a Function of Age|url= |journal=The Journal of Sexual Medicine|language=en|volume=6|issue=12|pages=3305–3319|doi=10.1111/j.1743-6109.2009.01489.x|pmid=19751384|issn=1743-6095|pmc=2978963}}</ref>

'''''Childhood Sexual Abuse:''''' Among the long-term symptoms found to exist decades following childhood sexual abuse (CSA) is a broad spectrum of sexual difficulties in adulthood. Meston has conducted a series of studies seeking to understand the mechanisms by which those sexual difficulties develop, including the degree to which the CSA is integrated into the individual’s sexual self-schema and the degree to which the individual identifies as being a victim of sexual abuse.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Rellini|first1=Alessandra H.|last2=Meston|first2=Cindy M.|date=2011-04-01|title=Sexual Self-Schemas, Sexual Dysfunction, and the Sexual Responses of Women with a History of Childhood Sexual Abuse|url= |journal=Archives of Sexual Behavior|language=en|volume=40|issue=2|pages=351–362|doi=10.1007/s10508-010-9694-0|issn=1573-2800|pmc=3047701|pmid=21140286}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Kilimnik|first1=Chelsea D.|last2=Meston|first2=Cindy M.|date=2019-03-01|title=Sexual Violence Identification and Women's Sexual Well-Being|url=https://doi.org/10.1007/s11930-019-00186-y|journal=Current Sexual Health Reports|language=en|volume=11|issue=1|pages=1–8|doi=10.1007/s11930-019-00186-y|s2cid=86461384|issn=1548-3592|url-access=subscription}}</ref> Based on these cognitive mechanisms, Meston developed an expressive writing intervention for sexual trauma that has been found to be efficacious in reducing sexual difficulties following CSA.<ref name=":7">{{Cite journal|last1=Meston|first1=Cindy M.|last2=Lorenz|first2=Tierney A.|last3=Stephenson|first3=Kyle R.|date=September 2013|title=Effects of expressive writing on sexual dysfunction, depression, and PTSD in women with a history of childhood sexual abuse: Results from a randomized clinical trial|journal=The Journal of Sexual Medicine|volume=10|issue=9|pages=2177–2189|doi=10.1111/jsm.12247|issn=1743-6095|pmc=3775987|pmid=23875721}}</ref>

'''''Psychometric Development:''''' Meston has developed a number of questionnaires and psychometric tools to measure constructs in sexuality ranging from sexual function to sexual satisfaction. She co-authored the Female Sexual Function Index,<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Rosen, C. Brown, J. Heiman, S. Leib|first=R.|date=April 2000|title=The Female Sexual Function Index (FSFI): A Multidimensional Self-Report Instrument for the Assessment of Female Sexual Function|journal=Journal of Sex & Marital Therapy|language=en|volume=26|issue=2|pages=191–208|doi=10.1080/009262300278597|pmid=10782451|s2cid=216086942 |issn=0092-623X|doi-access=free}}</ref> which is the most widely used psychometric measure of female sexual function. She also authored the ''Sexual Satisfaction Scale for Women'',<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Meston|first1=Cindy|last2=Trapnell|first2=Paul|date=January 2005|title=Development and Validation of a Five-Factor Sexual Satisfaction and Distress Scale for Women: The Sexual Satisfaction Scale for Women (SSS-W)|journal=The Journal of Sexual Medicine|volume=2|issue=1|pages=66–81|doi=10.1111/j.1743-6109.2005.20107.x|issn=1743-6095|pmc=2859306|pmid=16422909}}</ref> the ''Why Have Sex Questionnaire'',<ref name=":6" /> and the ''Cues for Sexual Desire Sca''le.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=McCall|first1=Katie|last2=Meston|first2=Cindy|date=September 2006|title=Cues Resulting in Desire for Sexual Activity in Women|journal=The Journal of Sexual Medicine|volume=3|issue=5|pages=838–852|doi=10.1111/j.1743-6109.2006.00301.x|issn=1743-6095|pmc=2861288|pmid=16942529}}</ref>

== Books ==
* Meston, C. M., & Buss, D. M. (2009). ''Why Women Have Sex: Sexual Motivation from Adventure to Revenge (and Everything in Between).'' New York: [[Henry Holt and Company]], Inc. (Translated into 9 languages).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Why Women Have Sex {{!}} Cindy M. Meston {{!}} Macmillan|url=https://us.macmillan.com/whywomenhavesex/cindymmeston/9780312662653|access-date=2021-01-04|website=US Macmillan|language=en-US}}</ref>
* Goldstein, I, Meston, C. M., Davis, S. R., & Traish, A. M. (Eds.) (2006). ''Women’s Sexual Function and Dysfunction''. London: [[Taylor & Francis]] Group.<ref name=":2" />

== Selected publications ==
* Meston, C. M., & Gorzalka, B. B. (1995).  The effects of sympathetic activation on physiological and subjective sexual arousal in women.  ''Behaviour Research and Therapy'', ''33'', 651-664.<ref name=":3" />
* Meston, C. M., & Frohlich, P. F. (2000). The neurobiology of sexual function.  ''Archives of General Psychiatry, 57,'' 1012-1030.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Meston|first1=Cindy M.|last2=Frohlich|first2=Penny F.|date=2000-11-01|title=The Neurobiology of Sexual Function|url=https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamapsychiatry/fullarticle/481665|journal=Archives of General Psychiatry|language=en|volume=57|issue=11|pages=1012–1030|doi=10.1001/archpsyc.57.11.1012|pmid=11074867|issn=0003-990X|url-access=subscription}}</ref>
* Meston, C. M., Rellini, A. H., & Heiman, J. R. (2006). Women’s history of sexual abuse, their sexuality, and sexual self-schemas.  ''Journal of Consulting and Clinical Psychology, 74,'' 229-236.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Meston|first1=Cindy M.|last2=Rellini|first2=Alessandra H.|last3=Heiman|first3=Julia R.|date=April 2006|title=Women's history of sexual abuse, their sexuality, and sexual self-schemas|journal=Journal of Consulting and Clinical Psychology|volume=74|issue=2|pages=229–236|doi=10.1037/0022-006X.74.2.229|issn=0022-006X|pmid=16649867}}</ref>
* Meston, C. M., & Buss, D. (2007). Why humans have sex. ''Archives of Sexual Behavior, 36,'' 477-507.<ref name=":6" />
* Lorenz, T. K., & Meston, C. M. (2012). Acute exercise improves physical sexual arousal in women taking antidepressants. ''Annals of Behavioral Medicine, 43,'' 352-361.<ref name=":5" />
* Meston, C. M., Lorenz, T, K. A., & Stephenson, K. R. (2013). Effects of expressive writing on sexual dysfunction, depression, and PTSD in women with a history of childhood sexual abuse: Results from a randomized clinical trial. ''Journal of Sexual Medicine, 10,'' 2177-2189''.''<ref name=":7" />
* Harte, C. B., Watts, T. W., & Meston, C. M. (2013). Predictors of 1-, 6-, and 12-month smoking relapse among smokers completing a tobacco cessation intervention program. ''Journal of Substance Abuse, 18, 405-416.'' <ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Harte|first1=Christopher B.|last2=Watts|first2=Tyler W.|last3=Meston|first3=Cindy M.|date=2013-10-01|title=Predictors of 1-, 6- and 12-month smoking cessation among a community-recruited sample of adult smokers in the United States|url=https://doi.org/10.3109/14659891.2012.709913|journal=Journal of Substance Use|volume=18|issue=5|pages=405–416|doi=10.3109/14659891.2012.709913|s2cid=18313478|issn=1465-9891|url-access=subscription}}</ref>
* Lorenz, T. K., & Meston, C. M. (2014) Exercise improves sexual function in women taking antidepressants: results from a randomized crossover trial. ''Depression and Anxiety, 31,'' 188-195.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Lorenz|first1=Tierney Ahrold|last2=Meston|first2=Cindy May|date=March 2014|title=Exercise improves sexual function in women taking antidepressants: Results from a randomized crossover trial|journal=Depression and Anxiety|volume=31|issue=3|pages=188–195|doi=10.1002/da.22208|issn=1091-4269|pmc=4039497|pmid=24754044}}</ref>
* Kilimnik, C. D., [[Ryan L. Boyd|Boyd, R. L.]], Stanton, A. M., & Meston, C. M. (2018). Identification of nonconsensual sexual experiences and the sexual self-schemas of women: Implications for sexual functioning''. Archives of Sexual Behavior, 47,'' 1633-1647.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Kilimnik|first1=Chelsea D.|last2=Boyd|first2=Ryan L.|last3=Stanton|first3=Amelia M.|last4=Meston|first4=Cindy M.|date=2018|title=Identification of Nonconsensual Sexual Experiences and the Sexual Self-Schemas of Women: Implications for Sexual Functioning|journal=Archives of Sexual Behavior|volume=47|issue=6|pages=1633–1647|doi=10.1007/s10508-018-1229-0|issn=1573-2800|pmid=29845443|s2cid=44069311}}</ref>
* Meston, C. M. & Stanton, A. S. (2018). Desynchrony between subjective and physiological sexual arousal in women: Theoretically interesting but clinically irrelevant. ''Current Sexual Health Reports,'' 1-3.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Meston|first1=Cindy M.|last2=Stanton|first2=Amelia M.|date=September 2018|title=Desynchrony Between Subjective and Genital Sexual Arousal in Women: Theoretically Interesting but Clinically Irrelevant|url=http://link.springer.com/10.1007/s11930-018-0155-4|journal=Current Sexual Health Reports|language=en|volume=10|issue=3|pages=73–75|doi=10.1007/s11930-018-0155-4|s2cid=52044008|issn=1548-3584|url-access=subscription}}</ref>
* Stanton, A. S., Boyd, R. L., Fogarty, J. J., & Meston, C. M. (2019). Heart rate variability [[biofeedback]] increases sexual arousal among women with female sexual arousal disorder: Results from a randomized-controlled trial. ''Behavior Research and Therapy, 115,'' 90-102.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Stanton|first1=Amelia M.|last2=Boyd|first2=Ryan L.|last3=Fogarty|first3=Justin J.|last4=Meston|first4=Cindy M.|date=2019|title=Heart rate variability biofeedback increases sexual arousal among women with female sexual arousal disorder: Results from a randomized-controlled trial|journal=Behaviour Research and Therapy|volume=115|pages=90–102|doi=10.1016/j.brat.2018.10.016|issn=1873-622X|pmid=30466714|s2cid=53721456 }}</ref>
* Meston, C. M., & Stanton, A. S. (2019). Understanding sexual arousal and subjective-genital arousal desynchrony in women. ''Nature Reviews Urology, 16,'' 107-120.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Meston|first1=Cindy M.|last2=Stanton|first2=Amelia M.|date=February 2019|title=Understanding sexual arousal and subjective–genital arousal desynchrony in women|url=https://www.nature.com/articles/s41585-018-0142-6|journal=Nature Reviews Urology|language=en|volume=16|issue=2|pages=107–120|doi=10.1038/s41585-018-0142-6|pmid=30664743|s2cid=58560008|issn=1759-4820|url-access=subscription}}</ref>
* Freihart, B. K.., & Meston, C. M. (2019). Preliminary evidence for a relationship between physiological synchrony and sexual satisfaction. ''Journal of Sexual Medicine, 16 (12),'' 2000 – 2010.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Freihart|first1=Bridget K.|last2=Meston|first2=Cindy M.|date=2019|title=Preliminary Evidence for a Relationship Between Physiological Synchrony and Sexual Satisfaction in Opposite-Sex Couples|journal=The Journal of Sexual Medicine|volume=16|issue=12|pages=2000–2010|doi=10.1016/j.jsxm.2019.09.023|issn=1743-6109|pmid=31708485|s2cid=207949903 }}</ref>
